coredata.dc:title: Sensitive detection of biothiols and histidine based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots–Hg(II) system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Juan
Zhang, Fengshuang
Yan, Xu
Wang, Long
Yan, Jin
Ding, Hong
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we presented a novel, rapid and highly sensitive sensor for glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and histidine (His) based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)–Hg(II) system. The CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted approach in one pot according to our previous report. The fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched in the presence of Hg(II) due to the coordination occurring between Hg(II) and functional groups on the surface of CQDs. Subsequently, the fluorescence of the CQDs–Hg(II) system was recovered gradually with the addition of GSH, Cys or His due to their stronger affinity with Hg(II). A good linear relationship was obtained from 0.10 to 20μmolL−1 for GSH, from 0.20 to 45μmolL−1 for Cys and from 0.50 to 60μmolL−1 for His, respectively. This method has been successfully applied to the trace detection of GSH, Cys or His in human serum samples with satisfactory results. The proposed method was simple in design and fast in operation, which demonstrated great potential in bio-sensing fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence detection
Bio-sensor
Biothiols
Histidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FL quenching of CQDs by Hg(II)
FL recovery of the CQDs–Hg(II) system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the detection process for GSH, Cys and His.

FL emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concertration (0–40μmolL−1) of Hg(II) in the 10mmolL−1 PBS buffer at pH 6.0. Inset is the relationship between F/F 0 and C Hg(II). F and F 0 are the FL intensities of CQDs in the presence and absence of Hg(II) at 440nm, respectively.

pH effect on the CQDs FL-quenched effiency by 25μmolL−1 Hg(II) and the CQDs–Hg(II) system FL recovered effiency in the presence of 25μmolL−1 GSH, Cys His or Trp in the 10mmolL−1 PBS buffer at pH 5.5, 6, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5, 9.0, 9.5.

FL restoration of the CQDs–25μmolL−1 Hg(II) system upon the addtion of different concentrations of (A) GSH from 0 to 20μmolL−1, (B) Cys from 0 to 45μmolL−1, (C) His from 0 to 60μmolL−1 in the 10mmolL−1 PBS bulffer at pH 6.0, respectively. Insets: the plot of F/F 0 versus the C GSH (0.1–20μmolL−1), C Cys (0.2–45μmolL−1) or C His (0.5–60μmolL−1), respectively. F is the recovered FL intensity of CQDs–25μmolL−1 Hg(II) system in the presence of GSH, Cys or His, and F 0 is the FL intensity of CQDs at 440nm, respectively.

Effect of 20μmolL−1 valine (Val), isoleucine (Iso), threonine (Thr), leucine (Leu), tyrosine (Tyr), phenylalanine (Phen), alanine (Ala), proline (Pro), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH) on the FL of CQDs quenched by 25μmolL−1 Hg(II).
Comparison of linear range and detect limit for GSH, Cys and His using different fluorescent probes.
Analytical results of the determination of GSH, Cys and His in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive detection of biothiols and histidine based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots–Hg(II) system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we presented a novel, rapid and highly sensitive sensor for glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys) and histidine (His) based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs)–Hg(II) system. The CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted approach in one pot according to our previous report. The fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched in the presence of Hg(II) due to the coordination occurring between Hg(II) and functional groups on the surface of CQDs. Subsequently, the fluorescence of the CQDs–Hg(II) system was recovered gradually with the addition of GSH, Cys or His due to their stronger affinity with Hg(II). A good linear relationship was obtained from 0.10 to 20μmolL−1 for GSH, from 0.20 to 45μmolL−1 for Cys and from 0.50 to 60μmolL−1 for His, respectively. This method has been successfully applied to the trace detection of GSH, Cys or His in human serum samples with satisfactory results. The proposed method was simple in design and fast in operation, which demonstrated great potential in bio-sensing fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the 20 amino acids utilized to make up proteins, cysteine (Cys) and histidine (His) play crucial roles for their participation in the process of biological functions [1]. Low-molecular-weight thiols, such as glutathione (GSH) and Cys are often involved in the reversible redox reactions and cellular functions including metabolism and detoxification [2–6]. Abnormal levels of GSH and Cys in human serum and urine are linked to many diseases, such as hematopoiesis decrease, hair depigmentation, lethargy, weakness, etc. His is also an essential bioactive amino acid in humans and other mammals. It controls the transport of metal elements as a neuromodulator or neurotransmitter in the central nervous system [6–8]. It has been reported that the deficiency of His in serum may result in an impaired nutritional state in patients with chronic kidney disease and pulmonary disease [7]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a sensitive and fast method for the determination of GSH, Cys and His in human serum samples.

So far, several methods have been reported to determine GSH, Cys and His in previous literatures, including capillary electrophoresis [9,10], spectrometry analysis [11,12], colorimetric analysis [2,13] and liquid/gas chromatography [14,15]. However, these methods usually suffer from more or less inherent drawbacks, such as long operation time, expensive instrumentation, complicated extraction procedures, etc. Recently, fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing attention owing to the operational simplicity, real-time detection, high-throughput process and good sensitivity [5,16–18].

Generally, fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have been practically designed for the detection of various substances in many fields [19]. However, heavy metals are essential elements in these conventional semiconductor materials, and new concerns have been raised over the potential toxicity and environmental hazards. Moreover, the preparation methods of QDs are often complicated and time-consuming. Therefore, a considerable effort has been spent in the search for benign nanomaterials with similar optical properties [20].

Carbon quantum dots (so-called CQDs), as newly emerging carbon-based materials, appear to be visible substitutes for QDs in many fields due to their advantages, for instance stable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, high fluorescent activity and excellent biocompatibility [21–25]. To date, various methods for CQDs synthesis have been well-developed [26], such as laser ablation, electrochemical release or exfoliation, chemical oxidation, thermal dehydration and proton-beam irradiation. However, most approaches involve intricate processes, rigorous experimental conditions or low production yields, consequently leading to inadequate applications of CQDs in potential analytical chemistry. Based on our previous report, we presented a one-pot strategy toward large-scale preparation of water-soluble CQDs with good PL [27]. To the best of our knowledge, the development of CQDs-based chemical (bio) analysis had a relatively late start compared with their intensive applications in bioimaging [22,28,29], and previous studies mostly focused on the CQDs-based sensing system for metal ions [21,30–32]. However, by taking full advantages of CQDs unique properties, optical sensors based on CQDs will achieve a rapid progress within the last several years.

In this paper, we developed a sensitive fluorescent detection assay for GSH, Cys and His based on the recovered FL intensity of the CQDs–Hg(II) system. The designed assay principle is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Owing to the coordination occurring between Hg(II) and functional groups on the surface of CQDs, the fluorescence was quenched efficiently. With the addition of GSH, Cys or His, the FL of the CQDs–Hg(II) system was recovered gradually because of their stronger affinities with Hg(II) [6,31]. Herein, CQDs were used as novel fluorescent materials for the rapid detection of GSH, Cys or His, respectively, which simultaneously avoided intricate preparation and surface modification. The biosensor showed a wide linear detection range, low detection limit and superior sensitivity, which was significant for both fundamental research and potential applications. Notably, this assay was successfully employed to the analysis of GSH, Cys and His in human serum samples.

All the starting materials of the CQDs synthesis were used without further purification. High purity water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm−1 was obtained from the United States Milli-Q purification system preparation. HgCl2 was purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp. (Beijing, China), and standard stock solutions of Hg(II) were prepared by dissolving HgCl2 in double-distilled water with the same concentration of HCl. Different concentrations of Hg(II) solutions were obtained by diluting standard stock solutions. Amino acids standards were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) of various pH were prepared with different ratios of Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, and ultrapure water. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp using right-angle geometry. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. In these experiments, a 1cm path-length quartz cuvette was used.

Water-soluble CQDs were synthesized according to our previous work [27]. Triammonium citrate (5g) and Na2HPO4 (1g) were dissolved in 10mL distilled water to form a transparent solution. The mixture was sonicated for 2min, and then heated in an ordinary household microwave oven at 750W for 2min 20s. A large number of brownish-yellow CQDs powders were obtained directly. The obtained powders were precipitated with ethanol–water (V:V, 80:20), and then centrifuged to remove impurities. No further purified procedure or surface passivation was required. In this work, CQDs were redissolved in double-distilled water to a final concentration of 10.0mgL−1 and stored at room temperature away from light. It should be pointed out that the starting materials of CQDs synthesis are non-toxic and environmentally friendly.

The FL detection was performed at room temperature in PBS buffer (pH 6.0). In a typical test, to study the quenching effect of Hg(II) on the FL intensity of CQDs, 20μL of CQDs solution, 100μL of 0.2molL−1 PBS buffer solution and different amounts of Hg(II) were added into a 4.0-mL centrifuge tube, and then diluted to 2.0mL with deionized water. Subsequently, the mixture was shaken thoroughly and equilibrated for 8min.

Then, we investigated the effect of GSH, Cys or His on the FL recovery of the CQDs–Hg(II) system. Herein, 20μL of CQDs solution, 100μL of 0.2molL−1 PBS buffer solution and a certain amount of Hg(II) were sequentially added into a 4.0-mL centrifuge tube, shaken thoroughly and equilibrated for 8min. Different amounts of analytes were added to the above mixture solution, diluted to 2.0mL with deionized water, shaken thoroughly and set for 2min. The final concentration of Hg(II) was 25μmolL−1.

All the FL detections were under the same conditions: the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 10nm, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the spectrophotometer by setting the excitation wavelength at 350nm. The FL intensities were recorded in the wavelength ranging from 380nm to 580nm, and the FL intensity of the maximum emission peak was used for quantitative analysis.

Fresh human serum samples were collected from three healthy adult volunteers with different ages at the First Hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, China) and stored frozen until assay. All the experiments were conducted in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and the relevant institutional committees have approved the experiments. The collected serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of GSH, Cys or His, respectively, and then incubated for 1h at room temperature. In order to eliminate the interferences of high proteins and improve recoveries, a 1mL serum sample was mixed with 1mL of acetonitrile to precipitate proteins [6,33]. After vortexing for 2min, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min, and then the clear supernatant layer was filtered through a 0.45μm Millipore filter for further sample analysis. Before measurement, the filtrate was appropriately diluted with 0.01molL−1 PBS (pH 6.0) in order to be consistent with the dynamic range of our method.

The fluorescence emission spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs are shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information). It could be seen that the typical absorption peak around 320–340nm represented the typical absorption of a graphitic structure. The FL spectrum of CQDs showed a maximum emission at 440nm upon excitation at 350nm, which was consistent with our previous report [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL quenching of CQDs by Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we investigated systematically the FL quenching of CQDs by Hg(II). The ratios of the FL intensity in the presence of Hg(II) (I) to the initial FL intensity (I 0) were plotted against response time (Fig. S2A, Supplementary Information). Hg(II) coordination with CQDs reached equilibrium at approximately 8min, and then remained constant. Thus, in the subsequent experiments, CQDs mixed with 25μmolL−1 Hg(II) were shaken thoroughly for 8min before the FL intensity was recorded.

Then, we also investigated the FL quenching capability of Hg(II) on the CQDs under optimal conditions. Fig. 1 displays the typical FL spectra as functions of the concentration of Hg(II) (C Hg(II)) from 0.1 to 40.0μmolL−1. The FL intensities of CQDs decreased obviously when Hg(II) solutions were added, and there was no shift in FL emission positions. The fluorescence was nearly completely quenched in the presence of 40.0μmolL−1 Hg(II), According to our previous report, CQDs in the absence of Hg(II) exhibited a strong FL emission peak at 440nm, and the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) was determined to be 14.3%. However, the QY decreased to 2.2% after the addition of 25μmolL−1 Hg(II). Inset in Fig. 1 shows the relative FL intensities (F/F 0) which exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg(II) (C Hg(II), μmolL−1) in the range from 0.1 to 25μmolL−1, following the regression equation:
(II)
F/F 0 =0.9916−0.0318 C Hg(II), μmolL−1 (1

The corresponding regression coefficient (R 2) was 0.9968, and the detection limit (LOD) for Hg(II) was 0.06μmolL−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for nine replicate measurements of 25μmolL−1 Hg(II) was 2.9%.

To determine the nature of the quenching process, the fluorescence lifetime measurements were also carried out. From two-exponential law of the fluorescence decay curves, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs decreased from 5.36ns in the absence of Hg(II) to 3.42 and 1.81ns in the presence of 1μmolL−1 and 5μmolL−1 Hg(II), respectively. Thus, the quenching was concluded to be dynamic in nature. It is speculated that both the quantum size effect and surface defects contribute to the FL of CQDs, and the luminescence emission of the CQDs arises from the radiative recombination of excitons [34]. Hg(II) was adsorbed onto the surface of CQDs as a surface modifier, and coordinated with the organic functional groups, which changed the surface traps or electron-hole recombination annihilation via electron or energy transfer process [31,35]. Therefore, the fluorescence could be quenched efficiently.
As a result, we chose 25μmolL−1 Hg(II) for the subsequent studies, and then GSH, Cys or His was introduced into the CQDs–Hg(II) system for the FL restoration.

The pH of the solution affected not only the fluorescent properties of pure CQDs solution [27], but also the fluorescent detection. FL intensity of pure CQDs was pH-dependent as reported in our previous study, but it did not change apparently in the pH range of 5.5–9.5. We investigated the pH effect on the FL-quenched efficiency by Hg(II) (Effq) and FL-recovered efficiency by GSH, Cys or His (Effr) in the PBS buffer (10mmolL−1) at pH 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5, 9.0 and 9.5, respectively. The efficiencies were calculated by the following equations, respectively:
(2) Effq (%)=(F 0 − F)/F 0
(3) Effr (%) =(F r − F)/(F 0 − F)
where F and F 0 represented the FL intensities of CQDs at 440nm in the presence and absence of Hg(II), respectively; F r was the recovered FL intensity of CQDs at 440nm in the presence of target analytes (or other amino acids) at the same pH value.

As shown in Fig. 2 , when 25μmolL−1 Hg(II) and CQDs solution were added into different PBS buffer solutions (10mmolL−1), the Effq increased with increasing the pH from 5.5 to 9.0 and then decreased. In acidic medium, the CQDs–Hg(II) complex was dissociated due to protonation of the carboxyls on the surface of CQDs, which resulted in a relatively lower Effq. With increasing the pH, the carboxylic groups on the surface of CQDs became deprotonated, and the combination of CQDs with Hg(II) was strengthened, leading to a higher quenching efficiency. However, at a high pH value (pH 9.5), Hg(II) would give priority to the combination with the alkaline groups in solution, which reduced its recombination with the function groups on the surface of CQDs and finally led to a lower Effq [31].

In contrast, Effr decreased gradually in the presence of 20μmolL−1 GSH, Cys and His with the increase of pH. In the case of Cys, Effr was lower than that of GSH but displayed the same change trend. Furthermore, tryptophan (Trp) had a fluorescence emission in the range of 300–400nm, and was found to interfere with the FL emission of CQDs. As evidenced in Fig. 2, the Effr of Trp displayed the opposite change tendency compared with that of GSH, Cys and His. At a high pH value (pH 9.0), Hg(II) presented the greatest Effq (93.7%), whereas the Effr was only 67.03% (for 20μmolL−1 GSH), 23.33% (for 20μmolL−1 Cys) and 15.7% (for 20μmolL−1 His). At the same time, the Effr of Trp at pH 9.0 appeared to be in the range of 7–8%, which had a larger interference. However, at pH 6.0, Effq still kept a relatively high value (79.3%), and Effr increased remarkably to 91.20% (for 20μmolL−1 GSH), 40.96% (for 20μmolL−1 Cys) and 26.12% (for 20μmolL−1 His). More importantly, the Effr of Trp (20μmolL−1) at pH 6.0 was only 2.02% and could be considered eliminated. Therefore, in order to eliminate the interference of Trp and obtain a better recovering condition, the pH was set at 6.0, which was also suitable for the application in biological fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL recovery of the CQDs–Hg(II) system
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous reports [31,35–37], some α-amino acids are strong Hg(II) chelators, such as GSH, Cys and His. They could form stable complexes with Hg(II), and thus Hg(II) was removed from the surface of CQDs, thereby recovering the FL of the CQDs–Hg(II) system. The FL intensity of pure CQDs had a negligible change in the presence of GSH, Cys and His. The FL recovery only resulted from forming Hg(II)–(amino acid) n complexes. Various α-amino acids at the same concentration had different restoration abilities to the CQDs–Hg(II) system. As shown in Fig. S2B (Supplementary Information), we found that the FL intensity of the CQDs–Hg(II) system could be restored by GSH, Cys or His (20μmolL−1), and remained constant after 2min.

In the presence of biothiols (such as GSH and Cys), the FL recovery was attributed to the strong binding preference between Hg(II) and the thiol groups, and the Hg(II) was detached from the surface of CQDs [6,35–38]. Therefore, the FL could be recovered. Fig. 3 (A) and (B) showed the FL spectra of the CQDs–Hg(II) system with different concentrations of GSH or Cys added. The restoration ability followed the order GSH>Cys which was similar to previous reports [2,35]. The relatively larger steric hindrance effect of GSH prevented Hg(II) from quenching the CQDs, and thereby, the Effr of GSH was always higher than that of Cys at the same concentration [35]. The FL of the system would be recovered with increasing the concentration of GSH from 0 to 20μmolL−1 (or Cys from 0 to 45μmolL−1). Insets displayed a good linear relationship between the FL intensity ratio (F/F 0) and the concentration of GSH (C GSH) (or C Cys) in the range of 0.1–20μmolL−1 for GSH and 0.2–45μmolL−1 for Cys. The regression equations were:
(4)
F/F 0 =0.2073+0.03634CGSH, μmolL−1, (R 2 =0.9985).
(5)
F/F 0 =0.2042+0.01627CCys, μmolL−1, (R 2 =0.9968).
F was the FL intensity of the CQDs–Hg(II) (25μmolL−1) system in the presence of GSH (or Cys), and F 0 was the FL intensity of pure CQDs. The LOD of GSH and Cys was 0.03μmolL−1 and 0.05μmolL−1, respectively. The RSD for nine replicate measurements of 2.0μmolL−1 GSH or Cys was 2.0% and 2.5%, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3(C), the introduction of His could also lead to a significant FL restoration of the CQDs–Hg(II) (25μmolL−1) system due to the strong coordination of imidazole side chains with Hg(II) [6,39]. The inset indicated a good linear relationship between the FL intensity ratio (F/F 0) and the His concentration (C His). The regression equation was:
(6)
F/F 0 =0.2082+0.01026 C His, μmolL−1, (R 2 =0.9952).
The linear range ranged from 0.5μmolL−1 to 60μmolL−1. The LOD and RSD of 2μmolL−1 His added were 0.15μmolL−1 and 2.8%, respectively.

This fluorescent detection assay utilized the novel, biocompatible materials CQDs as the FL probe. In a word, FL intensity of the CQDs–Hg(II) system was turned on because the side chains of GSH, Cys and His played a fundamental role. GSH, Cys and His appeared to be much stronger than other amino acids to compete for Hg(II) with the CQDs. However, the situation was more favorable to GSH and Cys in our proposed system. In addition, as observed from Table 1 , the dynamic ranges were comparable with previous fluorescence-based methods in recent years. The detection limit is lower or at least comparable to other sensors.

To verify the performance of our sensor system for GSH, Cys and His detection in practical applications, we investigated the effect of a series of physiological molecules on the FL of the present sensor system, such as 20μmolL−1 valine (Val), isoleucine (Iso), threonine (Thr), leucine (Leu), tyrosine (Tyr), phenylalanine (Phen), alanine (Ala), proline (Pro), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), tryptophan (Trp), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH). Fig. 4 shows the changes of the relative FL intensity (F/F 0) of the CQDs–Hg(II) (25μmolL−1) system. It could be observed that His Cys and GSH exhibited a significant enhancing effect on the FL intensity of CQDs–Hg(II) system. Although tyrosine (Tyr) exhibited a obviously quenching effect on the FL intensity of the system, it was formic acid that was used to dissolve the Tyr that played an important role in the quenching process and not Tyr [35]. Other amino acids had more or less positive or negative influences on the sensor system, but no tremendous fluorescence change was observed. In addition, the fluorescence recovery of the CQDs–Hg(II) system by GSH, Cys and His was also affected by less than 10% with the coexistence of other amino acids. These results indicated the diagnostic potential of the proposed sensor for detecting GSH, Cys or His in biological samples.

The feasibility of the proposed method for practical applications was investigated in the human serum samples. The serum samples were obtained from three healthy adult volunteers with different ages at the First Hospital of Jilin University in Changchun (China). Generally, appropriate pretreatment and dilution of serum (20-fold) were necessary to ensure the concentration of GSH, Cys and His in the linear range and obtain quantitative recoveries. For the recovery studies, the serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of GSH, Cys or His following the methodology described in Section 2.5. All of the unspiked serum samples gave a clear increase in the FL intensity of the CQDs–Hg(II) system. The concentrations of GSH, Cys or His in these samples were determined by the standard addition method and are listed in Table 2 . The concentrations determined in spiked samples by the developed method were in good agreement with GSH, Cys or His spiked, along with average quantitative recoveries ranging from 94 to 108% (Table 2). Furthermore, the RSD was lower than 3.8%. No significant interference with the determination in diluted samples was encountered after appropriate dilutions. Both detection limits and linear ranges are satisfactory. Moreover, the present approach is convenient and promising. The preliminary investigation demonstrated potential applicability in biological fluids of CQDs-based fluorescent turn-on sensor, and further detailed study with high selectivity is worth for in-depth research.

In summary, we have proposed a sensitive and rapid fluorescence turn-on assay to detect GSH, Cys or His using CQDs–Hg(II) system. Herein, the CQDs could be quenched by Hg(II) presumably via electron or energy transfer, and the mechanism has also been discussed. The FL intensity restoration is based on the highly specific interaction between Hg(II) and the amino acids or the strong fluorescent CQDs in a competitive assay format. In addition to favorable analytical performances, no further surface modification or purification of CQDs was required, which would greatly decrease the cost and simplify the processes. This sensor was simple in design, fast in operation and exhibited high sensitivity. What is more, such platform has been successfully employed to the detection of GSH, Cys and His in human serum samples with satisfactory results. We expect this strategy may offer a novel, convenient, and timely fluorescent probe for the detection of GSH, Cys and His in biological applications. More importantly, carbon-based materials should have a broad range of usage and desirable applications in the analytical areas especially in the development of bio-sensing systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (Grant number 20120327).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2014.12.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: G-quadruplex-based ultrasensitive and selective detection of histidine and cysteine
Colorimetric and fluorescent dual probe based on a polythiophene derivative for the detection of cysteine and homocysteine
A highly selective fluorescence turn-on sensor for cysteine/homocysteine and its application in bioimaging
A sensitive and selective resonance light scattering bioassay for homocysteine in biological fluids based on target-involved assembly of polyethyleneimine–capped Ag-nanoclusters
A one-step selective fluorescence turn-on detection of cysteine and homocysteine based on a facile CdTe/CdS quantum dots–phenanthroline system
Tyrosine-functionalized CuInS2 quantum dots as a fluorescence probe for the determination of biothiols, histidine and threonine
Ni2+-modified gold nanoclusters for fluorescence turn-on detection of histidine in biological fluids
An indicator-displacement assay for naked-eye detection and quantification of histidine in human urine
Highly sensitive simultaneous detection of cultured cellular thiols by laser induced fluorescence-capillary electrophoresis
Indirect electrochemiluminescence detection of lysine and histidine separated by capillary electrophoresis based on charge displacement
A simultaneous liquid chromatography/mass spectrometric assay of glutathione, cysteine, homocysteine and their disulfides in biological samples
Detection and analysis of protein histidine phosphorylation
Recent progress in fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of amino acids
Improved HPLC method for total plasma homocysteine detection and quantification
Analysis of amino acids in human serum by isocratic reverse-phase high-performance liquid chromatography with electrochemical detection
Caged rhodamine-based fluorescent probe for biothiol: selective detection of cysteine over homocysteine and glutathione in water
Selective detection of cysteine and glutathione using gold nanorods
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for specific detection of cysteine over homocysteine and glutathione based on the drastic distinction in the kinetic profiles
Preparation of surface imprinting polymer capped Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots and their application for chemiluminescence detection of 4-nitrophenol in tap water
A coumarin-based fluorescent probe for recognition of Cu2+ and fast detection of histidine in hard-to-transfect cells by a sensing ensemble approach
Graphene quantum dot as a green and facile sensor for free chlorine in drinking water
One-step synthesis of surface passivated carbon nanodots by microwave assisted pyrolysis for enhanced multicolor photoluminescence and bioimaging
Preparation of photoluminescent carbon nitride dots from CCl4 and 1,2-ethylenediamine: a heat-treatment-based strategy
Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum dots/graphene
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
Easy synthesis and imaging applications of cross-linked green fluorescent hollow carbon nanoparticles
Facile and green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles for cellular imaging
Carbon dots and chitosan composite film based biosensor for the sensitive and selective determination of dopamine

Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead(II) ions
